title: Lesson 337
transcript: Here's a topic that's been showing up in the exam recently, skimming. Unfortunately, probably many of us know what skimming is. Skimming is where an attacker captures your credit card or debit card information from a valid transaction. So this is basically where you go to pay for something in say a point of sale system or at a gas pump or maybe you hand your credit card to a serving person and your credit card information is captured. That's the whole idea of skimming. And here's some examples of what skimmers can look like. So here's one on an ATM machine. You can see it's like this extra little cover that goes on top of the ATM such that when you put your card in, it captures your card information. And the way they capture your pin is there's usually a little They'll paste what looks like a little mirror so you can see behind you on the ATM machine, but it actually has a camera in it that's looking down at the pin pad so they can capture your pin code as you type it in. And here's an example of what a skimmer would look like on a gas pump. You can see the image on the right. It's got this little extension on the end of the gas pump. That's the skimmer. Here's a really crafty example. This is an example of how organized crime is getting involved in this. Someone has spent a lot of money building and creating a plastic injection mold, creating a new cover for an Ingenico point of sale system that perfectly fits over top of the existing Ingenico point of sale system. And the way this works is that you can see there's this little tag down at the bottom of the point of sale system that says out of order. So the skimmers want you to use your less secure magnetic strip on your card. They want you to swipe your card through the reader, not put your card into the much more advanced Smart card capability so there they put that out of orders tag at the bottom Forcing you use the magnetic strip and if you flip this skimmer over you can see here when you slide your card through it captures your card information off the magnetic strip and then when you type in your pin code Your pin code is still registered on the actual point of system sale underneath, but this false cover also captures your code. This was a lot of effort to build this. Someone spent a lot of time and money building a plastic injection mold, silk screening this thing. It's a great example of how organized crime has gotten involved in skimming. So yeah, one more time, skimming is just this idea that an attacker captures your credit card information from a valid transaction.
